1:
"Now these are the nations which the LORD left, to prove Israel by them, even as many of Israel as had not known all the wars of Canaan;" The witnesses note the nations the LORD left in the land to prove Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes these nations are afterward mentioned. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the LORD leaving them to prove Israel, even as many as had not known the wars of Canaan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the nations the LORD left — in the land; the to prove Israel — a test; the had not known the wars — the new generation; the God refines by trial — the trial of your faith (1 Pet. 1:7).

The witnesses agree the nations were left "to prove Israel by them": what had been Israel's sin in failing to drive out the Canaanites, God now turned to a purpose of testing, that the new generation, who "had not known all the wars of Canaan," might be proved; the LORD overrules even his people's failures to try and refine their faith; it teaches that God tests his people by the trials and enemies he suffers to remain, making even the consequences of their sin serve his proving purpose, "that the trial of your faith... might be found unto praise" (1 Pet. 1:7), the nations left to prove Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse left to prove Israel: The witnesses note the the nations the LORD left: in the land. The witnesses note the to prove Israel: a test. The witnesses note the had not known the wars: the new generation. The witnesses note the God refines by trial: the trial of your faith (1 Pet. 1:7). The expositors observe the the nations the LORD left: in the land. The expositors observe the to prove Israel: a test. The expositors observe the had not known the wars: the new generation. The expositors observe the God refines by trial: the trial of your faith (1 Pet. 1:7). The believer is taught to mark the the nations the LORD left, the to prove Israel, the had not known the wars, the God refines by trial, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that the LORD left the nations to prove Israel — God overruling his people's failure to a purpose of testing, refining the new generation by trial, the trial of your faith found unto praise (1 Pet. 1:7).
2:
"Only that the generations of the children of Israel might know, to teach them war, at the least such as before knew nothing thereof;" The witnesses note that the nations were left to teach the young generations of Israel war. The witnesses note that Gill notes the nations were left that the young generations might by their wars learn the art of war and be inured to martial discipline. The witnesses note that this was for such as before knew nothing thereof. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the generations might know — the young; the to teach them war — the discipline of conflict; the knew nothing thereof — grown up in peace; the the school of warfare — fight the good fight of faith (1 Tim. 6:12).

The witnesses agree a further reason is given — "to teach them war": the new generation, who had grown up in the peace after the conquest, were to be kept in the discipline of conflict, that they might not grow soft and forgetful; the leaving of enemies to fight kept Israel watchful and dependent; it teaches that God keeps his people in the school of spiritual warfare, that they may not lose their hardihood in ease, the Christian life a continual conflict, "fight the good fight of faith" (1 Tim. 6:12); the believer too must learn war, putting on "the whole armour of God" (Eph. 6:11), the nations left to teach Israel war.

So the witnesses make the verse to teach them war: The witnesses note the the generations might know: the young. The witnesses note the to teach them war: the discipline of conflict. The witnesses note the knew nothing thereof: grown up in peace. The witnesses note the the school of warfare: fight the good fight of faith (1 Tim. 6:12). The expositors observe the the generations might know: the young. The expositors observe the to teach them war: the discipline of conflict. The expositors observe the knew nothing thereof: grown up in peace. The expositors observe the the school of warfare: fight the good fight of faith (1 Tim. 6:12). The believer is taught to mark the the generations might know, the to teach them war, the knew nothing thereof, the the school of warfare, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that the nations were left to teach Israel war — God keeping his people in the school of spiritual warfare lest they grow soft in ease, the believer learning war and putting on the whole armour of God (Eph. 6:11; 1 Tim. 6:12).
3:
"Namely, five lords of the Philistines, and all the Canaanites, and the Sidonians, and the Hivites that dwelt in mount Lebanon..." The witnesses note the nations left — the Philistine lords, Canaanites, Sidonians, and Hivites of Lebanon. The witnesses note that Gill notes the five Philistine lordships of Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, and Ekron; three had been taken by Judah but soon recovered. The witnesses note that the Sidonians and Hivites of Lebanon were among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the five lords of the Philistines — the unconquered; the Canaanites and Sidonians — the stubborn foes; the Hivites of Lebanon — remaining; the a lasting snare — thorns in your sides (Judg. 2:3).

The witnesses agree the catalogue names the chief of the unconquered peoples — "five lords of the Philistines... Canaanites... Sidonians... Hivites": these stubborn nations, some recovered after Judah's early victories, remained a standing trial and threat; the naming of the very foes that should have been driven out shows how Israel's incomplete obedience left them encompassed by enemies; it teaches that the sin or enemy not put away in the day of strength remains to vex and test in the day of weakness, the unconquered nations a lasting snare, "they shall be... thorns in your sides" (Judg. 2:3), the Philistines and Canaanites left in the land.

So the witnesses make the verse the nations named: The witnesses note the five lords of the Philistines: the unconquered. The witnesses note the Canaanites and Sidonians: the stubborn foes. The witnesses note the Hivites of Lebanon: remaining. The witnesses note the a lasting snare: thorns in your sides (Judg. 2:3). The expositors observe the five lords of the Philistines: the unconquered. The expositors observe the Canaanites and Sidonians: the stubborn foes. The expositors observe the Hivites of Lebanon: remaining. The expositors observe the a lasting snare: thorns in your sides (Judg. 2:3). The believer is taught to mark the five lords of the Philistines, the Canaanites and Sidonians, the Hivites of Lebanon, the a lasting snare, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that the unconquered nations — Philistines, Canaanites, Sidonians, Hivites — were left in the land — the foes that should have been driven out remaining as a standing trial, thorns in their sides (Judg. 2:3).
4:
"And they were to prove Israel by them, to know whether they would hearken unto the commandments of the LORD, which he commanded their fathers by the hand of Moses." The witnesses note the nations a test of whether Israel would obey the LORD's commandments. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were left to inure Israel to war, try their courage, and prove their faithfulness to God. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them proving whether Israel would hearken to the commandments. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the to prove Israel — a test of the heart; the to know — whether they would hearken; the the commandments of the LORD — by Moses; the the heart laid bare — to know whether ye love the LORD (Deut. 13:3).

The witnesses agree the deepest purpose of the test is named — "to know whether they would hearken unto the commandments of the LORD": the nations were left not chiefly to teach war but to prove the heart, whether Israel would keep God's law amid temptation; the trial would reveal what was in them, their obedience or their apostasy; it teaches that God proves his people to make manifest the state of their hearts, the test laying bare whether they truly love and obey him, "the LORD your God proveth you, to know whether ye love the LORD your God" (Deut. 13:3), the nations proving whether Israel would obey.

So the witnesses make the verse to prove their obedience: The witnesses note the to prove Israel: a test of the heart. The witnesses note the to know: whether they would hearken. The witnesses note the the commandments of the LORD: by Moses. The witnesses note the the heart laid bare: to know whether ye love the LORD (Deut. 13:3). The expositors observe the to prove Israel: a test of the heart. The expositors observe the to know: whether they would hearken. The expositors observe the the commandments of the LORD: by Moses. The expositors observe the the heart laid bare: to know whether ye love the LORD (Deut. 13:3). The believer is taught to mark the to prove Israel, the to know, the the commandments of the LORD, the the heart laid bare, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that the nations were to prove whether Israel would obey the LORD's commandments — God testing to make manifest the heart, the trial laying bare their love and obedience, to know whether ye love the LORD your God (Deut. 13:3).
5:
"And the children of Israel dwelt among the Canaanites, Hittites, and Amorites, and Perizzites, and Hivites, and Jebusites:" The witnesses note Israel dwelling among the Canaanite nations, as sojourners rather than conquerors. The witnesses note that Gill notes they dwelt among them as if only sojourners, not conquerors, by sufferance and not as owners, through their sloth and indolence. The witnesses note that they dwelt among the six nations of Canaan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Israel dwelt — among the Canaanites; the as sojourners — not conquerors; the through sloth — holding by sufferance; the not separate — come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:17).

The witnesses agree Israel sank to "dwelling among the Canaanites" — Gill notes, as if mere sojourners rather than conquerors, holding the land by sufferance through their own "sloth and indolence": instead of casting out the heathen, they settled down beside them; the mingling with the nations they should have driven out was the fruit of their failure and the seed of their fall; it teaches the peril of God's people making their home among the ungodly, conformed to the world rather than separate from it, the door opened to every temptation, "come out from among them, and be ye separate" (2 Cor. 6:17), Israel dwelling among the Canaanites.

So the witnesses make the verse dwelt among them: The witnesses note the Israel dwelt: among the Canaanites. The witnesses note the as sojourners: not conquerors. The witnesses note the through sloth: holding by sufferance. The witnesses note the not separate: come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:17). The expositors observe the Israel dwelt: among the Canaanites. The expositors observe the as sojourners: not conquerors. The expositors observe the through sloth: holding by sufferance. The expositors observe the not separate: come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:17). The believer is taught to mark the Israel dwelt, the as sojourners, the through sloth, the not separate, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Israel dwelt among the Canaanites as sojourners, not conquerors — the fruit of their failure, the peril of making their home among the ungodly, called instead to come out and be separate (2 Cor. 6:17).
6:
"And they took their daughters to be their wives, and gave their daughters to their sons, and served their gods." The witnesses note Israel intermarrying with the Canaanites and serving their gods. The witnesses note that Gill notes they intermarried contrary to God's express command, confounding their families, debasing their blood, and being ensnared into idolatry. The witnesses note that they served their gods. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took their daughters — to wife; the gave their daughters — intermarriage; the served their gods — idolatry followed; the the unequal yoke — what concord hath Christ with Belial (2 Cor. 6:14-15).

The witnesses agree the dwelling among the heathen bore its bitter fruit — "they took their daughters... gave their daughters... and served their gods": the forbidden intermarriage led straight to idolatry, as God had warned it would (Deut. 7:3-4); the unequal yoke drew their hearts after false gods; it teaches that unholy alliances with the ungodly corrupt the faith, the marriage-bond with unbelievers a sure road to apostasy, "be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers... what concord hath Christ with Belial?" (2 Cor. 6:14-15), Israel marrying the heathen and serving their gods.

So the witnesses make the verse served their gods: The witnesses note the took their daughters: to wife. The witnesses note the gave their daughters: intermarriage. The witnesses note the served their gods: idolatry followed. The witnesses note the the unequal yoke: what concord hath Christ with Belial (2 Cor. 6:14-15). The expositors observe the took their daughters: to wife. The expositors observe the gave their daughters: intermarriage. The expositors observe the served their gods: idolatry followed. The expositors observe the the unequal yoke: what concord hath Christ with Belial (2 Cor. 6:14-15). The believer is taught to mark the took their daughters, the gave their daughters, the served their gods, the the unequal yoke, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Israel intermarried with the Canaanites and served their gods — the forbidden union leading straight to idolatry, the unequal yoke a sure road to apostasy, what concord hath Christ with Belial (2 Cor. 6:14-15; Deut. 7:3-4).
7:
"And the children of Israel did evil in the sight of the LORD, and forgat the LORD their God, and served Baalim and the groves." The witnesses note Israel doing evil, forgetting the LORD, and serving Baalim and the groves. The witnesses note that Gill notes they did evil both by marrying with heathens and worshipping their gods. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set this the first apostasy bringing chastisement. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the did evil — in his sight; the forgat the LORD — the root of evil; the served Baalim — and the groves; the idols a snare — they forgat his works, served idols (Ps. 106:13, 36).

The witnesses agree the apostasy reached its height — "did evil... forgat the LORD their God, and served Baalim and the groves": forgetting the God who had redeemed and given them the land, they turned to Baal and Asherah, the idols of the heathen; the forgetting of God is the root of all their evil, the heart turned from him soon turning to idols; it teaches that to forget the LORD is the beginning of apostasy, and that the soul which loses the remembrance of God falls to serving false gods, "they soon forgat his works... and served their idols: which were a snare unto them" (Ps. 106:13, 36), Israel forgetting the LORD and serving Baalim.

So the witnesses make the verse forgat the LORD: The witnesses note the did evil: in his sight. The witnesses note the forgat the LORD: the root of evil. The witnesses note the served Baalim: and the groves. The witnesses note the idols a snare: they forgat his works, served idols (Ps. 106:13, 36). The expositors observe the did evil: in his sight. The expositors observe the forgat the LORD: the root of evil. The expositors observe the served Baalim: and the groves. The expositors observe the idols a snare: they forgat his works, served idols (Ps. 106:13, 36). The believer is taught to mark the did evil, the forgat the LORD, the served Baalim, the idols a snare, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Israel did evil, forgot the LORD, and served Baalim and the groves — the forgetting of God the root of all apostasy, the heart turned from him turning to idols, they soon forgat his works and served their idols (Ps. 106:13, 36).
8:
"Therefore the anger of the LORD was hot against Israel, and he sold them into the hand of Chushanrishathaim king of Mesopotamia: and the children of Israel served Chushanrishathaim eight years." The witnesses note the LORD's anger selling Israel into bondage to Chushan-rishathaim for eight years. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord's anger was hot because of their idolatry. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set this the first chastisement, the LORD selling them into the hand of the king of Mesopotamia. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the anger of the LORD — hot against them; the he sold them — into bondage; the Chushan-rishathaim — of Mesopotamia; the eight years — servitude for sin (Judg. 2:14).

The witnesses agree the apostasy brought swift chastisement — "the anger of the LORD was hot... he sold them into the hand of Chushanrishathaim": God gave his unfaithful people over to bondage, the very nations they had courted becoming their masters; the "selling" of Israel marks how God delivers up those who forsake him to the power of their enemies; it teaches that apostasy provokes God's anger and brings servitude, the forsaking of the LORD turning liberty into bondage, "the LORD... delivered them into the hands of spoilers that spoiled them" (Judg. 2:14), Israel sold into the hand of Chushan-rishathaim.

So the witnesses make the verse sold into bondage: The witnesses note the the anger of the LORD: hot against them. The witnesses note the he sold them: into bondage. The witnesses note the Chushan-rishathaim: of Mesopotamia. The witnesses note the eight years: servitude for sin (Judg. 2:14). The expositors observe the the anger of the LORD: hot against them. The expositors observe the he sold them: into bondage. The expositors observe the Chushan-rishathaim: of Mesopotamia. The expositors observe the eight years: servitude for sin (Judg. 2:14). The believer is taught to mark the the anger of the LORD, the he sold them, the Chushan-rishathaim, the eight years, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that the LORD's anger sold Israel into bondage to Chushan-rishathaim eight years — apostasy provoking his anger and bringing servitude, the forsaking of the LORD turning liberty into bondage, delivered into the hands of spoilers (Judg. 2:14).
9:
"And when the children of Israel cried unto the LORD, the LORD raised up a deliverer to the children of Israel, who delivered them, even Othniel the son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother." The witnesses note Israel crying to the LORD, who raised up Othniel as a deliverer. The witnesses note that Gill notes they cried toward the close of the eight years' bondage, groaning under their oppression. The witnesses note that Clarke sets the LORD raising up a deliverer, Othniel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Israel cried — in their misery; the the LORD raised up — a deliverer; the Othniel — caleb's younger brother; the God hears the cry — they cried, and he saved them (Ps. 107:13).

The witnesses agree that in their misery Israel "cried unto the LORD," and he, in mercy, "raised up a deliverer... Othniel": the crying of the afflicted people moved the compassion of God, who sent them a saviour; the raising-up of Othniel, the first of the judges, shows the readiness of God to deliver his people when they turn to him in their distress; it teaches that God hears the cry of his chastened people and sends deliverance, the deliverer his gift in answer to their cry, a figure of the great Deliverer raised up for a lost world, "they cried unto the LORD in their trouble, and he saved them out of their distresses" (Ps. 107:13), Othniel raised up to deliver Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse a deliverer raised up: The witnesses note the Israel cried: in their misery. The witnesses note the the LORD raised up: a deliverer. The witnesses note the Othniel: Caleb's younger brother. The witnesses note the God hears the cry: they cried, and he saved them (Ps. 107:13). The expositors observe the Israel cried: in their misery. The expositors observe the the LORD raised up: a deliverer. The expositors observe the Othniel: Caleb's younger brother. The expositors observe the God hears the cry: they cried, and he saved them (Ps. 107:13). The believer is taught to mark the Israel cried, the the LORD raised up, the Othniel, the God hears the cry, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Israel cried to the LORD, who raised up Othniel as a deliverer — God hearing the cry of his chastened people and sending salvation, the deliverer a figure of the great Deliverer, they cried and he saved them out of their distresses (Ps. 107:13).
10:
"And the Spirit of the LORD came upon him, and he judged Israel, and went out to war... and the LORD delivered Chushanrishathaim king of Mesopotamia into his hand..." The witnesses note the Spirit of the LORD coming upon Othniel, who judged Israel and overcame the oppressor. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Spirit moved him to the work, inspired him with courage, and filled him with every needful gift. The witnesses note that the LORD delivered Chushan-rishathaim into his hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Spirit of the LORD — came upon him; the he judged Israel — empowered; the went out to war — by divine enabling; the a type of Christ — the Spirit of the LORD upon him (Isa. 11:2; Luke 4:18).

The witnesses agree the deliverer was empowered from above — "the Spirit of the LORD came upon him": it was no strength of his own, but the Spirit who moved, inspired, and equipped Othniel for his work, that the victory might be God's; the coming of the Spirit upon the judge shows that all true deliverance and service flow from divine enabling; it teaches that the servants of God do his work only as his Spirit empowers them, the deliverer anointed for his task a foreshadowing of Christ, on whom "the Spirit of the LORD" rested (Isa. 11:2), and who came "to preach deliverance to the captives" (Luke 4:18), the Spirit coming upon Othniel.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit came upon him: The witnesses note the the Spirit of the LORD: came upon him. The witnesses note the he judged Israel: empowered. The witnesses note the went out to war: by divine enabling. The witnesses note the a type of Christ: the Spirit of the LORD upon him (Isa. 11:2; Luke 4:18). The expositors observe the the Spirit of the LORD: came upon him. The expositors observe the he judged Israel: empowered. The expositors observe the went out to war: by divine enabling. The expositors observe the a type of Christ: the Spirit of the LORD upon him (Isa. 11:2; Luke 4:18). The believer is taught to mark the the Spirit of the LORD, the he judged Israel, the went out to war, the a type of Christ, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that the Spirit of the LORD came upon Othniel, who overcame the oppressor — all true deliverance flowing from divine enabling, the anointed judge a foreshadowing of Christ on whom the Spirit rested, sent to preach deliverance to the captives (Luke 4:18; Isa. 11:2).
11:
"And the land had rest forty years. And Othniel the son of Kenaz died." The witnesses note the land resting forty years, until the death of Othniel. The witnesses note that Gill notes the forty years' rest from the time of this deliverance, by some reckoned to include the eight years' servitude. The witnesses note that Othniel the son of Kenaz died. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the land had rest — forty years; the through the deliverer — the blessing of restoration; the Othniel died — the deliverer mortal; the the unfailing Deliverer — he ever liveth to intercede (Heb. 7:25).

The witnesses agree the deliverance brought a long peace — "the land had rest forty years" — until the deliverer's death: the rest given through Othniel shows the blessing that follows when God's people are restored to him by his saviour; yet the rest lasted only while the deliverer lived, and the mention of his death foretells the relapse to come; it teaches that the peace of God's people is bound up with their faithfulness and with the deliverer he gives, and that earthly deliverers die, but points beyond to the rest that the unfailing Deliverer secures, "he ever liveth to make intercession" (Heb. 7:25), the land at rest forty years till Othniel died.

So the witnesses make the verse rest forty years: The witnesses note the the land had rest: forty years. The witnesses note the through the deliverer: the blessing of restoration. The witnesses note the Othniel died: the deliverer mortal. The witnesses note the the unfailing Deliverer: he ever liveth to intercede (Heb. 7:25). The expositors observe the the land had rest: forty years. The expositors observe the through the deliverer: the blessing of restoration. The expositors observe the Othniel died: the deliverer mortal. The expositors observe the the unfailing Deliverer: he ever liveth to intercede (Heb. 7:25). The believer is taught to mark the the land had rest, the through the deliverer, the Othniel died, the the unfailing Deliverer, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that the land had rest forty years until Othniel died — the peace that follows restoration to God, yet bound to the deliverer's life, pointing beyond to the unfailing Deliverer who ever liveth to make intercession (Heb. 7:25).
12:
"And the children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the LORD: and the LORD strengthened Eglon the king of Moab against Israel, because they had done evil in the sight of the LORD." The witnesses note Israel doing evil again, the LORD strengthening Eglon of Moab against them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they fell into idolatry again, a great evil and what they were prone to. The witnesses note that Henry introduces Ehud, the next of the judges. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the did evil again — the relapse; the the LORD strengthened Eglon — as a rod; the against Israel — for their sin; the the rod of his anger — o Assyrian, the rod of mine anger (Isa. 10:5).

The witnesses agree the sad cycle turns again — "the children of Israel did evil again... and the LORD strengthened Eglon... against" them: the relapse into idolatry brought a new oppressor, and it was the LORD himself who "strengthened" the heathen king as a rod against his sinning people; God's use of the enemy to chasten his own shows that even the oppressor's power is in his hand; it teaches that God may arm the wicked against his people when they sin, the rod of the ungodly wielded by his providence for their correction, "O Assyrian, the rod of mine anger" (Isa. 10:5), the LORD strengthening Eglon against Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse did evil again: The witnesses note the did evil again: the relapse. The witnesses note the the LORD strengthened Eglon: as a rod. The witnesses note the against Israel: for their sin. The witnesses note the the rod of his anger: O Assyrian, the rod of mine anger (Isa. 10:5). The expositors observe the did evil again: the relapse. The expositors observe the the LORD strengthened Eglon: as a rod. The expositors observe the against Israel: for their sin. The expositors observe the the rod of his anger: O Assyrian, the rod of mine anger (Isa. 10:5). The believer is taught to mark the did evil again, the the LORD strengthened Eglon, the against Israel, the the rod of his anger, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Israel did evil again, and the LORD strengthened Eglon against them — God using even the oppressor as his rod against his sinning people, the wicked the rod of his anger for their correction (Isa. 10:5).
13:
"And he gathered unto him the children of Ammon and Amalek, and went and smote Israel, and possessed the city of palm trees." The witnesses note Eglon allying with Ammon and Amalek, smiting Israel and taking the city of palm trees. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord inclined them, or the king of Moab got them to join, being his neighbours, in the war against Israel. The witnesses note that he possessed the city of palm trees. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the gathered Ammon and Amalek — a confederacy; the smote Israel — the chastened people; the the city of palm trees — jericho's region; the enemies grow bold — sold into the hands of enemies (Judg. 2:14).

The witnesses agree Eglon drew the neighbouring nations into a confederacy — "the children of Ammon and Amalek" — and "possessed the city of palm trees," the region of Jericho: the gathering of these old enemies of Israel against the chastened people shows how the foes of God's people combine when his hand is withdrawn; the taking of the palm-city, near where Israel first entered the land, marks how far they had fallen back; it teaches that when God's people forsake him, their enemies grow bold and prevail, the confederate heathen pressing in upon them, "the LORD... sold them into the hands of their enemies round about" (Judg. 2:14), Eglon taking the city of palm trees.

So the witnesses make the verse the city of palm trees: The witnesses note the gathered Ammon and Amalek: a confederacy. The witnesses note the smote Israel: the chastened people. The witnesses note the the city of palm trees: Jericho's region. The witnesses note the enemies grow bold: sold into the hands of enemies (Judg. 2:14). The expositors observe the gathered Ammon and Amalek: a confederacy. The expositors observe the smote Israel: the chastened people. The expositors observe the the city of palm trees: Jericho's region. The expositors observe the enemies grow bold: sold into the hands of enemies (Judg. 2:14). The believer is taught to mark the gathered Ammon and Amalek, the smote Israel, the the city of palm trees, the enemies grow bold, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Eglon allied with Ammon and Amalek and took the city of palm trees — the foes of God's people combining when his hand is withdrawn, enemies growing bold against the chastened, sold into the hands of enemies round about (Judg. 2:14).
14:
"So the children of Israel served Eglon the king of Moab eighteen years." The witnesses note Israel serving Eglon eighteen years, a severer chastisement for their relapse. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was ten years longer than they served the king of Mesopotamia, a severer correction for their relapse into idolatry. The witnesses note that Ellicott sets it an instance of their bondage. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the served Eglon — in bondage; the eighteen years — longer than before; the a severer correction — for the relapse; the the backslider chastened — if any draw back, no pleasure in him (Heb. 10:38).

The witnesses agree the bondage to Eglon was longer and harder than before — "eighteen years," Gill notes, "ten years longer" than the former servitude, "a severer correction... for their relapse": the heavier and longer chastisement answered the greater guilt of falling back after deliverance; the lengthening of the bondage shows that those who relapse into sin after mercy are visited with sharper discipline; it teaches that the backslider is dealt with more severely, the return to sin bringing a heavier rod, "if any man draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him" (Heb. 10:38), Israel serving Eglon eighteen years.

So the witnesses make the verse eighteen years: The witnesses note the served Eglon: in bondage. The witnesses note the eighteen years: longer than before. The witnesses note the a severer correction: for the relapse. The witnesses note the the backslider chastened: if any draw back, no pleasure in him (Heb. 10:38). The expositors observe the served Eglon: in bondage. The expositors observe the eighteen years: longer than before. The expositors observe the a severer correction: for the relapse. The expositors observe the the backslider chastened: if any draw back, no pleasure in him (Heb. 10:38). The believer is taught to mark the served Eglon, the eighteen years, the a severer correction, the the backslider chastened, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Israel served Eglon eighteen years — a severer and longer chastisement for their relapse, the backslider visited with a heavier rod, if any man draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him (Heb. 10:38).
15:
"But when the children of Israel cried unto the LORD, the LORD raised them up a deliverer, Ehud the son of Gera, a Benjamite, a man lefthanded..." The witnesses note Israel crying again, and the LORD raising up Ehud, a left-handed Benjamite, as deliverer. The witnesses note that Gill notes they cried after being long oppressed, brought to a sense of their sins and humiliation, asking forgiveness and deliverance. The witnesses note that Ehud was a left-handed man of Benjamin. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Israel cried — again; the the LORD raised up — ehud; the a Benjamite — left-handed; the the unlikely chosen — god chose the weak things (1 Cor. 1:27).

The witnesses agree God answered the renewed cry of his people with a new deliverer — "the LORD raised them up a deliverer, Ehud... a man lefthanded": the choosing of a left-handed man of Benjamin (whose name means "son of the right hand") shows God's way of using the unlikely and the seemingly disadvantaged for his purposes; the very trait that might seem a weakness became, in God's hand, the means of the deliverance; it teaches that God works by instruments the world would overlook, the weak made strong for his work, "God hath chosen the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty" (1 Cor. 1:27), Ehud the left-handed raised up to deliver Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse Ehud the left-handed: The witnesses note the Israel cried: again. The witnesses note the the LORD raised up: Ehud. The witnesses note the a Benjamite: left-handed. The witnesses note the the unlikely chosen: God chose the weak things (1 Cor. 1:27). The expositors observe the Israel cried: again. The expositors observe the the LORD raised up: Ehud. The expositors observe the a Benjamite: left-handed. The expositors observe the the unlikely chosen: God chose the weak things (1 Cor. 1:27). The believer is taught to mark the Israel cried, the the LORD raised up, the a Benjamite, the the unlikely chosen, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Israel cried and the LORD raised up Ehud, a left-handed Benjamite — God using the unlikely and seemingly disadvantaged, the weak made strong for his work, choosing the weak things to confound the mighty (1 Cor. 1:27).
16:
"And Ehud made him a dagger which had two edges, of a cubit length; and he did gird it under his raiment upon his right thigh." The witnesses note Ehud making a two-edged dagger and concealing it on his right thigh. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was a little sword with two edges, about half a yard long, which he could conceal, being left-handed. The witnesses note that he girt it on his right thigh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a dagger — two edges; the a cubit length — half a yard; the on his right thigh — hidden, being left-handed; the the two-edged sword — sharper than any twoedged sword (Heb. 4:12).

The witnesses agree Ehud prepared his instrument with care — "a dagger which had two edges... upon his right thigh": being left-handed, he hid the weapon where it would not be looked for, on the right side; the deliberate readying of the means shows the prudence joined to his resolve; the two-edged blade, hidden yet sharp, has been read as a faint figure of the word of God, "sharper than any twoedged sword" (Heb. 4:12), by which the message of judgment is carried home; it teaches that God's deliverer goes to his work prepared and resolute, the instrument of judgment made ready, Ehud girding the two-edged dagger.

So the witnesses make the verse the two-edged dagger: The witnesses note the a dagger: two edges. The witnesses note the a cubit length: half a yard. The witnesses note the on his right thigh: hidden, being left-handed. The witnesses note the the two-edged sword: sharper than any twoedged sword (Heb. 4:12). The expositors observe the a dagger: two edges. The expositors observe the a cubit length: half a yard. The expositors observe the on his right thigh: hidden, being left-handed. The expositors observe the the two-edged sword: sharper than any twoedged sword (Heb. 4:12). The believer is taught to mark the a dagger, the a cubit length, the on his right thigh, the the two-edged sword, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Ehud made and concealed a two-edged dagger — the deliverer prepared and resolute, the two-edged blade a faint figure of the word of God by which the message of judgment is carried home, sharper than any twoedged sword (Heb. 4:12).
17:
"And he brought the present unto Eglon king of Moab: and Eglon was a very fat man." The witnesses note Ehud bringing the tribute-present to Eglon, a very fat man. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was accompanied by servants who bore the presents, as became a judge of Israel. The witnesses note that Eglon was a very fat man. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the present — the tribute; the unto Eglon — the oppressor; the a very fat man — grown gross on the spoil; the ripe for judgment — prepared for the slaughter (Jer. 12:3).

The witnesses agree Ehud came as the bearer of Israel's tribute — "the present unto Eglon... a very fat man": the noting of the king's great fatness marks the ease and luxury in which the oppressor lived, grown gross on the spoil of God's people; the fat tyrant, secure in his prosperity, was ripe for judgment, like a beast fattened for the slaughter; it teaches that the prosperity of the wicked is no security, the rich oppressor fattened for the day of reckoning, "they are... as fed horses" yet brought down (cf. Jer. 12:3, prepare them for the slaughter), Eglon the very fat king receiving the present.

So the witnesses make the verse a very fat man: The witnesses note the the present: the tribute. The witnesses note the unto Eglon: the oppressor. The witnesses note the a very fat man: grown gross on the spoil. The witnesses note the ripe for judgment: prepared for the slaughter (Jer. 12:3). The expositors observe the the present: the tribute. The expositors observe the unto Eglon: the oppressor. The expositors observe the a very fat man: grown gross on the spoil. The expositors observe the ripe for judgment: prepared for the slaughter (Jer. 12:3). The believer is taught to mark the the present, the unto Eglon, the a very fat man, the ripe for judgment, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Ehud brought the present to Eglon, a very fat man — the oppressor grown gross in luxury on the spoil of God's people, the prosperous wicked ripe for judgment, prepared as for the day of slaughter (Jer. 12:3).
18:
"And when he had made an end to offer the present, he sent away the people that bare the present." The witnesses note Ehud, the present delivered, sending away the attendants who bore it. The witnesses note that Gill notes he had delivered the things and very probably made a speech to the king in the name of Israel. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him sending away the people that bore the present. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the made an end to offer — the present delivered; the sent away the people — the attendants; the to gain private access — his plan; the prudence in the work — a prudent man foreseeth (Prov. 22:3).

The witnesses agree Ehud, his errand seemingly done, "sent away the people that bare the present": the dismissing of his attendants was part of his plan to gain private access to the king, that the work of deliverance might be done alone; the careful ordering of his approach shows the wisdom with which he laid his design; it teaches that the servants of God may use prudence and strategy in their callings, ordering their steps with care to accomplish the work given them, "a prudent man foreseeth the evil" (Prov. 22:3), Ehud sending away the bearers of the present.

So the witnesses make the verse sent away the people: The witnesses note the made an end to offer: the present delivered. The witnesses note the sent away the people: the attendants. The witnesses note the to gain private access: his plan. The witnesses note the prudence in the work: a prudent man foreseeth (Prov. 22:3). The expositors observe the made an end to offer: the present delivered. The expositors observe the sent away the people: the attendants. The expositors observe the to gain private access: his plan. The expositors observe the prudence in the work: a prudent man foreseeth (Prov. 22:3). The believer is taught to mark the made an end to offer, the sent away the people, the to gain private access, the prudence in the work, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Ehud, the present delivered, sent away the attendants — wisdom in laying his design, the servant of God ordering his steps with care to accomplish the work, a prudent man foreseeing (Prov. 22:3).
19:
"But he himself turned again from the quarries that were by Gilgal, and said, I have a secret errand unto thee, O king: who said, Keep silence. And all that stood by him went out from him." The witnesses note Ehud returning to Eglon with word of a secret errand, and the king clearing the room. The witnesses note that Gill notes the quarries by Gilgal were places where stones were dug and hewn, as far as Ehud accompanied his men. The witnesses note that the king said, Keep silence, and all went out. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the turned again — to the king; the a secret errand — the pretext; the keep silence — the king's curiosity; the all went out — providence working (Job 5:13).

The witnesses agree Ehud gained his private audience by the word of "a secret errand" — and the king, his curiosity stirred, dismissed his attendants: "all that stood by him went out from him": the king's own command cleared the way for the deliverer, providence working through Eglon's desire for a secret message; the emptying of the room left the oppressor alone with the instrument of God's judgment; it teaches that God can turn the very wishes of the wicked to serve his purpose, the tyrant unwittingly preparing his own end, "he taketh the wise in their own craftiness" (Job 5:13), Eglon clearing the room at the word of a secret errand.

So the witnesses make the verse a secret errand: The witnesses note the turned again: to the king. The witnesses note the a secret errand: the pretext. The witnesses note the keep silence: the king's curiosity. The witnesses note the all went out: providence working (Job 5:13). The expositors observe the turned again: to the king. The expositors observe the a secret errand: the pretext. The expositors observe the keep silence: the king's curiosity. The expositors observe the all went out: providence working (Job 5:13). The believer is taught to mark the turned again, the a secret errand, the keep silence, the all went out, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Ehud returned with word of a secret errand, and Eglon cleared the room — God turning the very wishes of the wicked to serve his purpose, the tyrant unwittingly preparing his own end, he taketh the wise in their own craftiness (Job 5:13).
20:
"And Ehud came unto him; and he was sitting in a summer parlour, which he had for himself alone. And Ehud said, I have a message from God unto thee. And he arose out of his seat." The witnesses note Ehud coming to Eglon alone, declaring a message from God, at which the king arose. The witnesses note that Gill notes Eglon had retired into his summer parlour, smaller and more private, where he used to be alone. The witnesses note that Ehud said, I have a message from God unto thee. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came unto him — alone; the the summer parlour — private; the a message from God — of judgment; the he arose — none stand before his word (Heb. 4:12).

The witnesses agree Ehud declared his true errand — "I have a message from God unto thee" — and the king "arose out of his seat": the message from God was a message of judgment, and at its announcement even the heathen king rose, as if in awe; the rising of Eglon to hear a word from God, though it was his doom, shows that the word of God commands reverence even from the ungodly; it teaches that God sends his messages of judgment as surely as of mercy, and that none can stand before his word, the oppressor risen to meet the message of his end, "the word of God... is a discerner of the thoughts" (Heb. 4:12), Eglon arising at the message from God.

So the witnesses make the verse a message from God: The witnesses note the came unto him: alone. The witnesses note the the summer parlour: private. The witnesses note the a message from God: of judgment. The witnesses note the he arose: none stand before his word (Heb. 4:12). The expositors observe the came unto him: alone. The expositors observe the the summer parlour: private. The expositors observe the a message from God: of judgment. The expositors observe the he arose: none stand before his word (Heb. 4:12). The believer is taught to mark the came unto him, the the summer parlour, the a message from God, the he arose, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Ehud declared a message from God, at which Eglon arose — the word of God commanding reverence even from the ungodly, his message of judgment as sure as of mercy, none standing before the discerning word (Heb. 4:12).
21:
"And Ehud put forth his left hand, and took the dagger from his right thigh, and thrust it into his belly:" The witnesses note Ehud, with his left hand, drawing the hidden dagger and slaying Eglon. The witnesses note that Gill notes being left-handed, he could the better do this without the king suspecting, who would have no fear at the moving of his left hand. The witnesses note that he thrust it into his belly. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the his left hand — unsuspected; the took the dagger — from his right thigh; the thrust it into his belly — the judgment; the the oppressor slain — he shall bring on them their iniquity (Ps. 94:23).

The witnesses agree the deliverance was wrought by the left hand the king never feared — "Ehud put forth his left hand, and took the dagger... and thrust it into his belly": the very thing in Ehud that seemed a weakness, his left-handedness, was the means by which the judgment was carried home unsuspected; the slaying of the oppressor was the execution of God's sentence upon a cruel tyrant by his appointed deliverer; it teaches that God brings down the proud oppressor in his time, and uses the unlikely instrument to do it, the message from God proving a sword of judgment, "the LORD shall... slay the wicked" (cf. Ps. 94:23, he shall bring upon them their own iniquity), Ehud slaying Eglon with the hidden dagger.

So the witnesses make the verse thrust it into his belly: The witnesses note the his left hand: unsuspected. The witnesses note the took the dagger: from his right thigh. The witnesses note the thrust it into his belly: the judgment. The witnesses note the the oppressor slain: he shall bring on them their iniquity (Ps. 94:23). The expositors observe the his left hand: unsuspected. The expositors observe the took the dagger: from his right thigh. The expositors observe the thrust it into his belly: the judgment. The expositors observe the the oppressor slain: he shall bring on them their iniquity (Ps. 94:23). The believer is taught to mark the his left hand, the took the dagger, the thrust it into his belly, the the oppressor slain, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that Ehud, with the left hand the king never feared, slew Eglon — God bringing down the proud oppressor by an unlikely instrument, the message from God a sword of judgment, bringing upon the wicked their own iniquity (Ps. 94:23).
22:
"And the haft also went in after the blade; and the fat closed upon the blade, so that he could not draw the dagger out of his belly..." The witnesses note the dagger driven in to the hilt, the fat closing over the blade. The witnesses note that Gill notes the handle as well as the blade went in, so strong and violent was the thrust, he determining to do his business effectually. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the haft going in after the blade. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the haft went in — after the blade; the the fat closed — over it; the done effectually — to the hilt; the prosperity his undoing — the prosperity of fools destroys them (Prov. 1:32).

The witnesses agree the blow was struck home with full resolve — "the haft also went in after the blade; and the fat closed upon the blade": Ehud, Gill notes, "determining to do his business effectually," drove the weapon to the hilt; the thoroughness of the deed marks the completeness with which God's judgment fell on the oppressor; the very fat of the luxurious king, swallowing the blade, became the token of his ruin; it teaches that when God executes judgment it is complete and sure, the deliverer doing his work effectually, the oppressor's prosperity itself turned to his undoing, "the prosperity of fools shall destroy them" (Prov. 1:32), the dagger lost in Eglon's fat.

So the witnesses make the verse the haft went in: The witnesses note the the haft went in: after the blade. The witnesses note the the fat closed: over it. The witnesses note the done effectually: to the hilt. The witnesses note the prosperity his undoing: the prosperity of fools destroys them (Prov. 1:32). The expositors observe the the haft went in: after the blade. The expositors observe the the fat closed: over it. The expositors observe the done effectually: to the hilt. The expositors observe the prosperity his undoing: the prosperity of fools destroys them (Prov. 1:32). The believer is taught to mark the the haft went in, the the fat closed, the done effectually, the prosperity his undoing, and to cry unto the LORD his deliverer. The verse thus records that the dagger was driven in to the hilt, the fat closing over the blade — God's judgment complete and sure, the deliverer doing his work effectually, the oppressor's very prosperity turned to his undoing, the prosperity of fools destroying them (Prov. 1:32).
23:
"Then Ehud went forth through the porch, and shut the doors of the parlour upon him, and locked them." The witnesses note Ehud going out through the porch and locking the parlour doors behind him. The witnesses note that Gill notes the porch, a place with many seats where people waited or the guards sat. The witnesses note that he shut and locked the doors of the parlour. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the went forth — through the porch; the shut the doors — of the parlour; the locked them — to gain time; the composed in danger — a sound mind (2 Tim. 1:7).

The witnesses agree Ehud made his escape with calm presence of mind — "went forth through the porch, and shut the doors... and locked them": the locking of the doors behind him gave him time before the deed was discovered; the coolness and prudence with which he withdrew show a man composed in the very moment of danger; it teaches that God gives his servants self-possession and wisdom in the hour of need, the deliverer ordering even his retreat with care, "the LORD shall give thee... the spirit of... a sound mind" (cf. 2 Tim. 1:7), Ehud locking the doors and going forth.

So the witnesses make the verse locked the doors: The witnesses note the went forth: through the porch. The witnesses note the shut the doors: of the parlour. The witnesses note the locked them: to gain time. The witnesses note the composed in danger: a sound mind (2 Tim. 1:7). The expositors observe the went forth: through the porch. The expositors observe the shut the doors: of the parlour. The expositors observe the locked them: to gain time. The expositors observe the composed in danger: a sound mind (2 Tim. 1:7). The believer is taught to mark the went forth, the shut the doors, the locked them, the composed in danger, and to follow the LORD who delivereth his people. The verse thus records that Ehud went out and locked the doors behind him — calm presence of mind in the hour of danger, God giving his servants self-possession and wisdom, the spirit of a sound mind (2 Tim. 1:7).
24:
"When he was gone out, his servants came; and when they saw that, behold, the doors of the parlour were locked, they said, Surely he covereth his feet in his summer chamber." The witnesses note the servants, finding the doors locked, supposing the king was at his ease. The witnesses note that Gill notes the servants came to the parlour door to reassume their place and finish their business with the king. The witnesses note that they supposed he covered his feet in his summer chamber. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the servants came — to the door; the the doors locked — they waited; the he covereth his feet — their supposition; the providential delay — the king's heart in the LORD's hand (Prov. 21:1).

The witnesses agree the servants' natural supposition gave Ehud his needed time — "they said, Surely he covereth his feet in his summer chamber": thinking the king at his ease and privacy, they waited rather than entering, and so the deed lay long undiscovered; the providential delay, born of their innocent assumption, served the deliverer's escape; it teaches that God orders even the small thoughts and delays of men to further his purposes, the servants' waiting a part of his providence, "the king's heart is in the hand of the LORD... he turneth it whithersoever he will" (Prov. 21:1), the servants supposing the king at his ease.

So the witnesses make the verse they supposed: The witnesses note the the servants came: to the door. The witnesses note the the doors locked: they waited. The witnesses note the he covereth his feet: their supposition. The witnesses note the providential delay: the king's heart in the LORD's hand (Prov. 21:1). The expositors observe the the servants came: to the door. The expositors observe the the doors locked: they waited. The expositors observe the he covereth his feet: their supposition. The expositors observe the providential delay: the king's heart in the LORD's hand (Prov. 21:1). The believer is taught to mark the the servants came, the the doors locked, the he covereth his feet, the providential delay, and to follow the LORD who delivereth his people. The verse thus records that the servants, finding the doors locked, supposed the king at his ease — the providential delay born of their assumption serving the deliverer's escape, God ordering even men's small thoughts, turning hearts as he will (Prov. 21:1).
25:
"And they tarried till they were ashamed: and, behold, he opened not the doors of the parlour; therefore they took a key, and opened them: and, behold, their lord was fallen down dead on the earth." The witnesses note the servants at last opening the doors and finding their lord dead. The witnesses note that Gill notes they tarried, not knowing what to think, or what should be the meaning of the doors kept locked so long. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them finding their lord fallen dead. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the tarried till ashamed — the long waiting; the took a key — and opened; the their lord dead — the judgment revealed; the the mighty brought low — desolation in a moment (Ps. 73:19).

The witnesses agree the truth came out at last — "they took a key, and opened them: and, behold, their lord was fallen down dead": the long waiting ended in the discovery of the slain king, the judgment of God upon the oppressor now revealed; the falling of the mighty tyrant, found dead upon the earth, shows how suddenly the great are brought low; it teaches that the proud oppressor, secure in his palace, is brought down in a moment by the hand of God, his end coming when least expected, "how are they brought into desolation, as in a moment!" (Ps. 73:19), their lord found fallen dead.

So the witnesses make the verse fallen down dead: The witnesses note the tarried till ashamed: the long waiting. The witnesses note the took a key: and opened. The witnesses note the their lord dead: the judgment revealed. The witnesses note the the mighty brought low: desolation in a moment (Ps. 73:19). The expositors observe the tarried till ashamed: the long waiting. The expositors observe the took a key: and opened. The expositors observe the their lord dead: the judgment revealed. The expositors observe the the mighty brought low: desolation in a moment (Ps. 73:19). The believer is taught to mark the tarried till ashamed, the took a key, the their lord dead, the the mighty brought low, and to follow the LORD who delivereth his people. The verse thus records that the servants opened and found their lord fallen dead — the judgment of God on the oppressor revealed, the proud tyrant brought low in a moment, how are they brought into desolation, as in a moment (Ps. 73:19).
26:
"And Ehud escaped while they tarried, and passed beyond the quarries, and escaped unto Seirath." The witnesses note Ehud escaping while the servants tarried, reaching Seirath in safety. The witnesses note that Gill notes the tarrying of the servants gave Ehud time to make his escape, out of reach of pursuers. The witnesses note that he passed beyond the quarries and escaped to Seirath. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the escaped — while they tarried; the beyond the quarries — past pursuit; the unto Seirath — in safety; the God preserves his own — preserve thy going out (Ps. 121:8).

The witnesses agree the delay of the servants secured the deliverer's flight — "Ehud escaped while they tarried... unto Seirath": the providential pause gave him time to reach safety beyond pursuit; the safe escape of the man who had done God's work shows the care of God over his servants; it teaches that the LORD preserves those who do his will, covering their flight and bringing them through danger, the deliverer kept safe to lead his people, "the LORD shall preserve thy going out and thy coming in" (Ps. 121:8), Ehud escaping to Seirath.

So the witnesses make the verse Ehud escaped: The witnesses note the escaped: while they tarried. The witnesses note the beyond the quarries: past pursuit. The witnesses note the unto Seirath: in safety. The witnesses note the God preserves his own: preserve thy going out (Ps. 121:8). The expositors observe the escaped: while they tarried. The expositors observe the beyond the quarries: past pursuit. The expositors observe the unto Seirath: in safety. The expositors observe the God preserves his own: preserve thy going out (Ps. 121:8). The believer is taught to mark the escaped, the beyond the quarries, the unto Seirath, the God preserves his own, and to follow the LORD who delivereth his people. The verse thus records that Ehud escaped to Seirath while the servants tarried — the providential delay securing his flight, God preserving those who do his will, the LORD preserving his going out and his coming in (Ps. 121:8).
27:
"And it came to pass, when he was come, that he blew a trumpet in the mountain of Ephraim, and the children of Israel went down with him from the mount, and he before them." The witnesses note Ehud blowing the trumpet in mount Ephraim, and Israel rallying to follow him. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was come to Seirath in the tribe of Ephraim. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him blowing a trumpet, and Israel going down with him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the blew a trumpet — the call; the in mount Ephraim — the rally; the Israel went down — answering; the he before them — the leader gathering (Joel 2:15-16).

The witnesses agree the deliverer now openly summoned the people — "he blew a trumpet in the mountain of Ephraim, and the children of Israel went down with him": the trumpet call rallied the oppressed to rise, and they followed their leader, "he before them"; the readiness of the people to answer the call shows how God's deliverance stirs his people to action; it teaches that the LORD raises up leaders and sounds the call to his people, who are to rise and follow, the trumpet of deliverance gathering the host, "blow ye the trumpet in Zion... gather the people" (Joel 2:15-16), Ehud blowing the trumpet and Israel following.

So the witnesses make the verse blew a trumpet: The witnesses note the blew a trumpet: the call. The witnesses note the in mount Ephraim: the rally. The witnesses note the Israel went down: answering. The witnesses note the he before them: the leader gathering (Joel 2:15-16). The expositors observe the blew a trumpet: the call. The expositors observe the in mount Ephraim: the rally. The expositors observe the Israel went down: answering. The expositors observe the he before them: the leader gathering (Joel 2:15-16). The believer is taught to mark the blew a trumpet, the in mount Ephraim, the Israel went down, the he before them, and to follow the LORD who delivereth his people. The verse thus records that Ehud blew the trumpet and Israel rallied to follow him — God's deliverance stirring his people to action, the leader raised up and the people following, blow the trumpet in Zion, gather the people (Joel 2:15-16).
28:
"And he said unto them, Follow after me: for the LORD hath delivered your enemies the Moabites into your hand. And they went down after him, and took the fords of Jordan..." The witnesses note Ehud leading Israel, declaring the LORD had delivered the Moabites, and seizing the fords of Jordan. The witnesses note that Gill notes he said this to encourage them, putting himself at their head, ready to expose his own life. The witnesses note that Clarke notes they took the fords of Jordan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the follow after me — the leader's call; the the LORD hath delivered — the word of faith; the your enemies into your hand — the victory claimed; the took the fords — if God be for us (Rom. 8:31).

The witnesses agree Ehud led with a word of faith — "Follow after me: for the LORD hath delivered your enemies... into your hand": the ground of his confidence was not their strength but God's delivering, the victory claimed by faith before it was won; the seizing of "the fords of Jordan" cut off the enemy's retreat, making the victory complete; it teaches that the leaders of God's people are to go before them in faith, assuring them of God's help, and that faith lays hold of the victory God promises, "the LORD hath delivered" the sure word of triumph, "if God be for us, who can be against us?" (Rom. 8:31), Ehud leading Israel to take the fords of Jordan.

So the witnesses make the verse follow after me: The witnesses note the follow after me: the leader's call. The witnesses note the the LORD hath delivered: the word of faith. The witnesses note the your enemies into your hand: the victory claimed. The witnesses note the took the fords: if God be for us (Rom. 8:31). The expositors observe the follow after me: the leader's call. The expositors observe the the LORD hath delivered: the word of faith. The expositors observe the your enemies into your hand: the victory claimed. The expositors observe the took the fords: if God be for us (Rom. 8:31). The believer is taught to mark the follow after me, the the LORD hath delivered, the your enemies into your hand, the took the fords, and to follow the LORD who delivereth his people. The verse thus records that Ehud led Israel in faith, the LORD having delivered the Moabites, and took the fords of Jordan — the leader going before in faith, the victory claimed before it was won, if God be for us, who can be against us (Rom. 8:31).
29:
"And they slew of Moab at that time about ten thousand men, all lusty, and all men of valour; and there escaped not a man." The witnesses note the slaughter of ten thousand Moabites, none escaping. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were sent into Israel to keep it in subjection, or had settled there for their convenience and profit. The witnesses note that there escaped not a man. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the slew ten thousand — of Moab; the all lusty — men of valour; the none escaped — the complete rout; the the foe wholly broken — the mighty shall fall (Jer. 8:2).

The witnesses agree the victory was complete — "they slew of Moab... about ten thousand men, all lusty, and all men of valour; and there escaped not a man": the whole occupying force, strong and valiant though they were, fell before Israel, none escaping; the totality of the overthrow shows how, when God delivers, the mightiest enemy is wholly broken; it teaches that the LORD makes the victory of his people complete, the strength and valour of the foe availing nothing against his hand, "the mighty shall fall... and none shall gather them" (cf. Jer. 8:2), ten thousand of Moab slain, none escaping.

So the witnesses make the verse none escaped: The witnesses note the slew ten thousand: of Moab. The witnesses note the all lusty: men of valour. The witnesses note the none escaped: the complete rout. The witnesses note the the foe wholly broken: the mighty shall fall (Jer. 8:2). The expositors observe the slew ten thousand: of Moab. The expositors observe the all lusty: men of valour. The expositors observe the none escaped: the complete rout. The expositors observe the the foe wholly broken: the mighty shall fall (Jer. 8:2). The believer is taught to mark the slew ten thousand, the all lusty, the none escaped, the the foe wholly broken, and to follow the LORD who delivereth his people. The verse thus records that ten thousand Moabites, strong and valiant, were slain, none escaping — the complete victory God gives, the mightiest foe wholly broken when he delivers, the mighty falling and none gathering them (Jer. 8:2).
30:
"So Moab was subdued that day under the hand of Israel. And the land had rest fourscore years." The witnesses note Moab subdued and the land resting eighty years. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Moabites in the land of Israel were broken, so weakened they could not bring Israel into subjection again for a long time. The witnesses note that the land had rest eighty years. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moab subdued — under Israel; the the oppressor broken — their power gone; the the land had rest — eighty years; the a rich peace — great peace have they which love thy law (Ps. 119:165).

The witnesses agree the deliverance brought a long peace — "Moab was subdued... and the land had rest fourscore years": the breaking of the oppressor gave Israel rest for eighty years, the longest peace in the book; the length of the rest shows the abundance of God's mercy following the deliverance; it teaches that God's salvation brings a long and rich peace to his people when they are restored to him, the rest the fruit of his deliverance, "great peace have they which love thy law" (Ps. 119:165), the land at rest eighty years.

So the witnesses make the verse rest eighty years: The witnesses note the Moab subdued: under Israel. The witnesses note the the oppressor broken: their power gone. The witnesses note the the land had rest: eighty years. The witnesses note the a rich peace: great peace have they which love thy law (Ps. 119:165). The expositors observe the Moab subdued: under Israel. The expositors observe the the oppressor broken: their power gone. The expositors observe the the land had rest: eighty years. The expositors observe the a rich peace: great peace have they which love thy law (Ps. 119:165). The believer is taught to mark the Moab subdued, the the oppressor broken, the the land had rest, the a rich peace, and to follow the LORD who delivereth his people. The verse thus records that Moab was subdued and the land had rest eighty years — the abundance of God's mercy following the deliverance, a long and rich peace the fruit of his salvation, great peace have they which love thy law (Ps. 119:165).
31:
"And after him was Shamgar the son of Anath, which slew of the Philistines six hundred men with an ox goad: and he also delivered Israel." The witnesses note Shamgar slaying six hundred Philistines with an ox goad and delivering Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes that after the death of Ehud, when Israel was in distress again, Shamgar was raised up of God to be a judge and deliverer. The witnesses note that he slew six hundred Philistines with an ox goad. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Shamgar — raised up of God; the six hundred Philistines — slain; the with an ox goad — a humble means; the he also delivered Israel — no restraint to save by few (1 Sam. 14:6).

The witnesses agree God wrought a great deliverance by the humblest of means — "Shamgar... slew of the Philistines six hundred men with an ox goad": a farmer's tool in the hand of a man God raised up did the work of an army; the using of so mean a weapon shows that God needs no great means to save, the victory plainly his when the instrument is so weak; it teaches that the LORD can deliver his people by few or by feeble means, the ox goad in Shamgar's hand a witness that "there is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few" (1 Sam. 14:6); thus the chapter closes, Shamgar also delivering Israel, that the glory might be God's alone, "God hath chosen the weak things... that no flesh should glory" (1 Cor. 1:27-29).

So the witnesses make the verse an ox goad: The witnesses note the Shamgar: raised up of God. The witnesses note the six hundred Philistines: slain. The witnesses note the with an ox goad: a humble means. The witnesses note the he also delivered Israel: no restraint to save by few (1 Sam. 14:6). The expositors observe the Shamgar: raised up of God. The expositors observe the six hundred Philistines: slain. The expositors observe the with an ox goad: a humble means. The expositors observe the he also delivered Israel: no restraint to save by few (1 Sam. 14:6). The believer is taught to mark the Shamgar, the six hundred Philistines, the with an ox goad, the he also delivered Israel, and to follow the LORD who delivereth his people. The verse thus records that Shamgar slew six hundred Philistines with an ox goad and delivered Israel — God working great deliverance by the humblest means, no restraint to save by many or by few, the glory his alone (1 Sam. 14:6; 1 Cor. 1:27-29).
